u2l5.l2: "I didn't go on Erasmus because I was scared, I think.
u2l5.l3: Afraid of being far away, afraid of leaving the school system I know and am used to!" Person 2: financial reasons. "I didn't take part in the Erasmus program because I couldn't afford it.
u2l5.l4: I think it's not a program accessible to everyone — rather to students from well-off families." Person 3: administrative procedures. "I tried to go abroad a year ago but the paperwork was too long and too complicated for me."
u2l5.l6: "We already struggle with classes in French... so studying in another language!" / "It's surprising, this stubbornness to deny the language barrier!
u2l5.l7: Except maybe for a few students, it's still really hard to follow classes in a foreign language." When and where did you go on an Erasmus program?
u2l5.l8: That year, were there many students from your class who went abroad?
u2l5.l10: I imagine the experience is beneficial, but here it is — it must not match my personality.
u2l5.l11: Me, I didn't take part in the Erasmus program because I couldn't afford it.
u2l5.l13: I tried to go abroad a year ago, but the administrative procedures were too long and too complicated for me.
u2l5.l14: So I gave up on the project.
u2l5.l15: I prefer to finish my studies at the same school and go abroad afterwards.
u2l5.l16: It's surprising, this stubbornness to deny the language barrier!
u2l5.l17: I was on Erasmus in Milan two years ago.
u2l5.l18: I had to fight to go, because officially I wasn't entitled to.
u2l5.l19: But I wanted to go at any cost and then do a master's at a grande école.
u2l5.l20: From my class, only two people went.
u2l5.l21: The experience was wonderful, and on top of that we got to choose the classes we wanted!
u2l5.l22: As for the grant, I wasn't entitled to that either.
u2l5.l23: But I found fairly easily a part-time job at an Italian company.

u2l7.l1: Person discriminated against because of age (either too young or too old); someone ill; the second person is victim of discrimination due to disability (her wheelchair can't fit through the classroom door); the third person is victim of racial discrimination (often barred from a nightclub because of ethnic origin). 3. Victims of discrimination can go to the Halde website — the High Authority for the Fight against Discrimination and for Equality. Me, I wanted to do an internship at a company but they told me they gave priority to employees' children.
u2l7.l3: My sister Marie can't go to the local school.
u2l7.l4: She's disabled and the school refused to enroll her because she can't fit into the classroom in her wheelchair.
u2l7.l5: Me, I have two buddies: Jules and Akim.
u2l7.l6: When we go to a club, often Akim can't get in.
u2l7.l9: If you think you've been discriminated against, go to the Halde website, the High Authority for the Fight against Discrimination and for Equality. Hello, this is Place à vous.
u2l7.l10: This week, we're talking about discrimination in access to housing with the Halde.
u2l7.l11: Let's take the case of Lionel, a Guadeloupean student who came to the mainland to study: he's therefore looking for an apartment.
u2l7.l12: Because his parents, who were acting as guarantors, lived in Guadeloupe, his application was refused.
u2l7.l13: I imagine it's just one example among many at the housing division you head, Fabien Dechavanne?
u2l7.l16: Starting from Lionel's case, the Halde noted that this type of refusal was ultimately quite frequent.
u2l7.l17: It asked the government to change the law.
u2l7.l19: From now on, the law expressly forbids refusing a rental to someone because their parents live overseas.
u2l7.l20: Since the law was amended, has the Halde been seized with other cases?
u2l7.l21: This type of behavior has clearly decreased.
u2l7.l22: When the Halde is confronted with it again, it reminds people of the law's provisions.
u2l7.l23: If the behavior persists, the case can be brought before the court.
u2l7.l25: We remind everyone that one can petition the Halde via the form on the website halde.fr.

u3l9.l1: The reason why the members of the band like this style of music. The opinion of the grandmother of one of them about this musical style. The band's objective for the Fête de la Musique.
u3l9.l2: "As soon as we were born, we heard Deep Purple.
u3l9.l3: Instead of Au clair de la lune, we listened to rock..." "AC/DC, Trust, Metallica, or even Nirvana." She's thrilled: "These four lads in the wind remind her of her youth"; To make noise: "And the singer fully intends to make some at the Fête de la Musique"; "to fire up the crowd on stage."
u3l9.l4: Formed barely a year ago, the Atomic Boys are young — very young.
u3l9.l6: On stage, his head barely rises above the snare drum, but rock — he has it in his blood.
u3l9.l7: After that, we... we had trouble listening to anything else because we were really into it, all that... so there you go." Their stage act is perfectly polished; for that, they draw inspiration from videos of their idols: AC/DC, Trust, Metallica, or even Nirvana.
u3l9.l8: And to guarantee the show, Frédéric, the band's singer, always gives 100%.
u3l9.l9: "When I sing, I do the stage act, I jump everywhere... uh... shouting... uh... I strip to a T-shirt and sometimes bare-chested, it's that hot." Pierrette is the rock'n'roll grandma of Johan the guitarist, always hanging around her place to rehearse.
u3l9.l10: These four lads in the wind remind her of her youth.
u3l9.l11: "I listen and listen and I'm thrilled.
u3l9.l12: When they're all there with the... the kids, then we put on the music and everything shakes.
u3l9.l13: Well, it makes a lot of noise.
u3l9.l14: No, but we don't dare too much because since I'm in an apartment, we have to think a bit about the neighbors.
u3l9.l15: They're not at all steeped in rock, these neighbors.
u3l9.l16: To our regret, really — because it's got to shred, as they say, it's got to make noise." And the singer fully intends to make noise at the Fête de la Musique.
u3l9.l18: Just as well, it suits AC/DC covers to a T.
u3l9.l19: So this weekend, he has only one desire: "to let loose, fire up the crowd on stage, and show the audience what I'm capable of."
u3l9.l20: So the rendezvous is set this weekend in Montbéliard — it's going to blow you away.
u3l9.l21: Germain Arrigoni and France Bleu Belfort-Montbéliard, for France Info.

u3l10.l1: He loves the guitar and gypsy jazz invented by Django Reinhardt. Are there questions that Thomas Dutronc doesn't particularly appreciate?
u3l10.l4: "It's that people constantly talk to him about his parents." "Some, like Mathieu Chédid, encourage him to sing them." I'm listening to some soul music — you may remember this hit by Françoise Hardy.
u3l10.l5: The transition is just right to introduce our guest — her son, Thomas Dutronc, who excels in a completely different genre: gypsy jazz.
u3l10.l6: And since this year, he sings, with a first album and a series of concerts.
u3l10.l9: What he doesn't like either, he says, is when people constantly talk to him about his parents.
u3l10.l10: That doesn't stop him from peppering his songs with nods to his father...
u3l10.l11: Live like Lennon... and it doesn't stop him either from modeling with mom for a mail-order catalog.
u3l10.l12: What he loves is the guitar and gypsy jazz — that typically French jazz invented in the 1920s by Django Reinhardt and Stéphane Grappelli.
u3l10.l13: And for about ten years now, he's made it his job.
u3l10.l14: A jazz guitarist, he has strung together concert tours with his musician friends, most of them met in Corsica, where he lives most of the time.
u3l10.l15: And since Thomas also has lots of friends in chanson, and writes chansons, some — like Mathieu Chédid — encourage him to sing them.
u3l10.l16: "It's true that through the... through the instrument alone, it's not easy to have as privileged a contact as you have with a song, and then quite simply he felt like singing — it doesn't explain itself any more complicated than that, you know...
u3l10.l17: Between Stéphane Grappelli and Django Reinhardt — between the guitarist and the violinist — well, it works or it doesn't, but when it works with a background like that, that says it all.
u3l10.l18: "I think we're on the trail of that friendship." The story of Thomas Dutronc is that of a true passion: for music and for his friends, who are, he says, his second family. You're in charge this month of updating the Music section of your school's blog.
u3l10.l19: Following the model of those about Olivia Ruiz and Thomas Dutronc, draft a profile of the artist your neighbor presented to you.

u6l21.l1: SOME RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CONSTRUCTING OR RESTRUCTURING PREMISES: Here are some recommendations concerning food-product hygiene.
u6l21.l3: First of all, one must avoid having the premises where preparation takes place open directly onto the outside.
u6l21.l4: The premises must have a sufficient number of hand-washing stations near the various workstations and at the exits of the restrooms.
u6l21.l5: It's important that waste be stored in closed bins.
u6l21.l6: Separate the peeling and washing areas (dirty sector) from the other product-handling areas (clean sector).
u6l21.l8: Windows must be kept closed during production and/or fitted with insect-protection screens.

u7l26.l1: Statement b best summarizes the second part of the recording, because one can hear that "the boundaries between the company and the home are becoming porous."
u7l26.l2: What's developing is the fact that... we work both at the office and at home.
u7l26.l3: What's developing is the fact that we're increasingly itinerant.
u7l26.l4: And so, in reality, the most important point is that location constraints on our activities are disappearing.
u7l26.l5: You can work... uh... in your car, in traffic.
u7l26.l7: You can work at your vacation spot.
u7l26.l8: You can work in a hotel, while traveling, by connecting... uh... to the network.
u7l26.l9: Our contacts don't know where we are.
u7l26.l10: So that's what makes the big difference, and from that point of view, it's true that the Internet changes not only the possibilities of working in one way or another, but contributes to the modification of lifestyles.
u7l26.l11: Which is also an important point from the standpoint of work.
u7l26.l12: I think the model we imagined twenty years ago, where people were going to stay home working and not come to the office anymore, hasn't developed at all.
u7l26.l14: I think what characterizes, uh, this domain is much more that the boundaries between the company and the home are becoming porous, you know...
u7l26.l15: And so people work partly at home, you know.
u7l26.l16: Perhaps, conversely, they do certain activities that touch on their private life at the company.
u7l26.l17: So the boundary between the professional world and private space is being transformed.

u7l27.l1: Four people are interviewed: Thierry, Chantal, Yves, and Cécile.
u7l27.l2: Thierry is interviewed because he checks his e-mails and works on his professional files during his time off.
u7l27.l3: As for Chantal and Yves, they're hooked on their BlackBerry, which accompanies them even to the beach.
u7l27.l4: Cécile is a workplace psychologist and speaks about work addiction.
u7l27.l5: Thierry thinks he has a moral duty toward his clients.
u7l27.l6: Chantal answers the phone at any moment to help her colleagues or clients.
u7l27.l7: Yves feels obliged to answer his e-mails at any moment.
u7l27.l8: The psychologist thinks it's a strong need for professional recognition.
u7l27.l9: Checking e-mails, following up on files — for Thierry, the word "vacation" doesn't rhyme with the word "loafing."
u7l27.l11: I think you can't explain to a client that his problem will be handled at the end of your own vacation.
u7l27.l12: So at some point, you have to take your responsibilities and keep working.
u7l27.l13: And for Chantal and Yves, on the beach, one single tool: the BlackBerry.
u7l27.l14: I have my BlackBerry; I can be called at any time — during a visit to a... to a castle, or when I'm at the beach, to be asked a question and asked for my assistance.
u7l27.l15: At first, you're very pleased because there are lots of things, you're on the Internet, you look at your stuff, and in the end you do nothing but e-mails in the morning, at noon, in the evening, on vacation, on weekends.
u7l27.l16: You're... you feel obligated to reply.
u7l27.l17: So where does this addiction to work come from?
u7l27.l19: It's a big search, generally, for professional recognition.
u7l27.l20: Often people tell me "Oh yes, but my employer is very demanding."
u7l27.l21: And then all the friction side one can actually have at work creates stress, you know, among... among people — hence they have trouble, in fact, disconnecting, you know, for just a few days of vacation, if you will.
u7l27.l22: Boss pressure, quest for recognition — for the work-addicted, this summer's vacation is anything but restful.

u9l33.l1: "Now, we're no longer very far from the people we love, who are even far away.
u9l33.l2: With the Internet, we have the image of the person we love right at home.
u9l33.l3: And the phone — it's very practical not to have to go home in the evening to get your messages but to have them live." "It's still a faster means of communication, but one that also allows us to be much freer, and to be available not only to your contacts but for yourself.
u9l33.l4: You have an immediate question to ask — you have the answer right away." "It separates people.
u9l33.l5: We're less inclined to go over to the person to speak to them; we're less inclined to wait for them to reflect before getting an answer.
u9l33.l6: It's changed our way of life, but we ourselves, as humans, still have to keep adapting." "I find that we're more and more assailed, and that we spend, without wanting to and in spite of ourselves, more and more time on the Internet." "...the intrusion of our work colleagues 24/7.
u9l33.l7: Yes, it can be dangerous because it can indeed disrupt a family life.
u9l33.l8: It's also dangerous when one is permanently disturbed by untimely phone calls." "Anyone will be able to know at what time I went into such-and-such shop, what I did, who I called, where I was... with GPS, with... with messages recorded on machines, all that.
u9l33.l9: People will know everything about everyone: where, when, and soon how...
u9l33.l10: In short, no one will have any privacy anymore." In your opinion, how have mobile phones or computers changed our lifestyles?
u9l33.l11: Cell phones and computers will of course change my life and communication habits.
u9l33.l12: They've changed everyone's life and communication habits, in fact.
u9l33.l13: There's good and bad, I think.
u9l33.l14: The good is that... uh... now we're no longer very far from the people we love, who are even far away.
u9l33.l15: Nevertheless, I also find that it separates people.
u9l33.l16: In the end, it's still a faster means of communication, but one that also allows us to be much freer, and to be available not only to your contacts but for yourself.
u9l33.l17: Indeed, I... I really agree with you.
u9l33.l18: That is, that's the very, very positive point: being able to have a precise answer quickly.
u9l33.l19: But on the other hand, I find that we're more and more assailed, and that we spend, without wanting to and in spite of ourselves, more and more time on the Internet.
u9l33.l20: Do you think the widespread adoption of these means of communication is dangerous for our privacy?
u9l33.l22: For example, the intrusion of our work colleagues 24/7.
u9l33.l23: Is it dangerous for our privacy?
u9l33.l25: When I see what we're able to do today with a cell phone.
u9l33.l26: You can take, use fingerprints to... to recognize the user.
u9l33.l27: We tell ourselves that maybe one day we'll use it as a means of payment.
u9l33.l28: You'll walk out of a store with just your cell phone in your pocket, and it'll pay for your shopping.
u9l33.l30: Anyone will be able to know at what time I went into such-and-such shop, what I did, who I called, where I was... with GPS, with... with messages recorded on machines, all that.
u9l33.l33: But we're intruding on people's private lives — that's for sure.

u9l34.l2: He is president of the Nicolas Hulot Foundation, whose mission is to make us aware of environmental problems and develop strategies to safeguard the planet.
u9l34.l3: Help people develop daily gestures that preserve the environment.
u9l34.l4: Encourage companies to change their modes of production in order to reduce their [footprint]. Help people develop daily gestures that preserve the environment.
u9l34.l5: So you... you chair your foundation, the Nicolas Hulot Foundation, and a year ago you launched, with ADEME — the Agency for the Environment and Energy Management — the Défi pour la Terre (Challenge for the Earth).
u9l34.l6: Can you remind us... remind us what it's about.
u9l34.l7: And so the idea is that each person choose, from these hundreds of gestures we make available to them day by day, well, gestures they commit to, in order to weigh less heavily on our natural resources and less heavily on our energy resources, of which we've just again been reminded that, alas, all of it is counted.
u9l34.l8: And we have been living for a very long time in a somewhat wasteful civilization, thinking we had infinite resources.
u9l34.l9: Well, it's time we realized that, alas, we live on a planet that is limited, and that we can't ask it for more than it can give us — so we must regulate that.
u9l34.l10: And we, as individuals, have a share of responsibility.
u9l34.l11: We don't have all the responsibility, but we can't off-load it by telling ourselves we can't do much.
u9l34.l12: In fact, we can do enormously much.
u9l34.l13: And, on top of that, we can do it while protecting the environment and gaining — I'd even say — economically for ourselves, and at the same time I'd even say gaining psychologically.
u9l34.l14: Because whether we like it or not, we are still conditioned in our consumption gestures, and even in our behaviors.
u9l34.l15: One of the most promising sectors right now is the green jobs oriented toward ecology.
u9l34.l16: Example with the company GreenFace, specialized in construction and eco-renovation.
u9l34.l19: Today, Ahmed Akaaboun is the boss of a Parisian company with about ten employees.
u9l34.l20: Barely a year and a half old, and GreenFace is going to open a branch in the south of France and continue its development.
u9l34.l21: Despite this period of crisis, today we have an order book that is filled through January.
u9l34.l22: This year, we've hired seven people, and for September we'll be brought to hire more than twenty. [There's a training between two and five days, but otherwise a standard painter or a standard mason can perfectly well use ecological materials.] An eco-friendly construction process, for the employees, means re-learning their trade in part, with healthy techniques and materials.
u9l34.l23: At 52, André Pochard has found a job again, but under better conditions.
u9l34.l24: I arrived here, well, checking off ads.
u9l34.l25: It's quite complicated currently, with the job market, well, that's not going too well.
u9l34.l27: Me, I'm a bit of the old generation, and I learned on the job, shall we say — there you go.
u9l34.l28: We're stopping everything that's oil-based paint, with turpentine, etc. It's still more pleasant for us.
u9l34.l29: For now, these hires are being done via a temp agency, also specialized in green trades.
u9l34.l30: Temp contracts that could give way to permanent jobs, according to Ahmed Akaaboun, once eco-construction has reached its cruising rhythm.

u10l38.l1: She decides to explain the reasons for her judgment.
u10l38.l2: "In museums, there's always such a crowd that you're constantly jostled or bothered by others." "Her china service is so ugly that it doesn't surprise me she gave it to the Louvre museum." "I always end my visit at the museum shop, so much so that I walk out with super-expensive objects that always end up at the bottom of a drawer." There are things one doesn't admit in society if one wishes to be respected.
u10l38.l3: Recently, at my friend Marie-Odile's birthday, when I said quite loudly, "Museums are boring," I immediately felt in the stares that I came across as an idiot.
u10l38.l4: After this incident, I therefore decided — not to do my mea culpa, but to argue my case: I can't bear anyone.
u10l38.l7: I can't bring myself to swoon in front of Madame René Coty's wedding tableware.
u10l38.l8: I have nothing against this lady.
u10l38.l9: But frankly, her china service is so ugly that it doesn't surprise me she donated it to the Louvre museum.
u10l38.l10: I always end my visit at the museum shop, so much so that I walk out with super-expensive objects that always end up at the bottom of a drawer.

u10l39.l1: Make citizens more aware "of the beauty, fragility, and grandeur of these monuments."
u10l39.l2: Let them discover the monuments that are the seat of the nation's authorities.
u10l39.l3: Force the public authorities to become aware of the richness of the national heritage and thus unlock funds.
u10l39.l4: Give recognition to the anonymous people who defend this heritage day by day.
u10l39.l5: He would have liked to make entry to national museums free — "at least on certain days or certain evenings" — in order to make them more democratic places.
u10l39.l6: I wanted the citizens themselves to become more aware of the beauty, the fragility, the grandeur of these monuments.
u10l39.l7: Hence the idea of opening, once a year and free of charge, all of these places.
u10l39.l8: And from the very first minute, it was a huge success.
u10l39.l9: A passion seized a lot of people.
u10l39.l10: Uh... it must be said that some of these monuments are monuments normally closed to the public.
u10l39.l11: I'm thinking in particular of those that are the seat of political and public authorities: the presidency of the Republic, the Hôtel Matignon, the ministries.
u10l39.l12: It is normal that the country's citizens, who, moreover, contribute through their taxes to financing indirectly or directly the restoration of these monuments, should be able to have access to them.
u10l39.l13: These monuments are national — that is, they belong to...
u10l39.l14: They are the collective property of the country.
u10l39.l15: That was... that was one of the reasons.
u10l39.l16: Another reason that led me to organize this event: I wanted, through this popular endorsement, for the public authorities — and notably the Ministry of Finance — to become aware that this is a great wealth for a country like ours and that one must not spare one's material efforts... uh... to avoid letting this heritage wither away.
u10l39.l17: And then one last reason: I wanted all those who, in anonymity — curators of... of monuments, Architects of Monuments of France, craftspeople... uh... businesses — who day after day work to save or restore, be in a way rewarded by this recognition... uh... national and popular.
u10l39.l18: And for them, you know, it's each year an encouragement to deploy themselves even better, to fight with energy to... uh... accomplish the work that is theirs.
u10l39.l19: With this event, Mr. Lang, can one speak, in your opinion, of democratization of cultural practices, of culture accessible to the greatest number?
u10l39.l20: Yes, so, if you will, me, I have one little regret.
u10l39.l21: I regret not having decided to make entry to national museums free.
u10l39.l22: When you have to come in a group of three, four, five, six, it's expensive, and that's a very important obstacle.
u10l39.l23: The English have done it for a long time, and the atmosphere of English museums is, I find, much warmer, much more... uh... simple... uh... friendly.
u10l39.l24: I think we should do it at least on certain days or certain evenings.
u10l39.l25: (Thanks to Mr. Jack Lang, MP and former minister.) Exclusive interview carried out for Taxi! 3.

u11l42.l1: A study by the site Expedia.fr is not very flattering for French tourists when they go abroad: they are neither generous nor very well-mannered.
u11l42.l2: It's the third year in a row that we're at the back of the pack.
u11l42.l3: There are still, Sophie Jousselin, serious efforts to be made.
u11l42.l4: Yes, especially since this year it's even worse.
u11l42.l6: This year we're 27th, dead last behind the Greeks, Turks, and Spaniards.
u11l42.l7: The study conducted among 4,500 hotels around the world doesn't go easy on us.
u11l42.l8: First complaint: we speak foreign languages little and badly.
u11l42.l9: Worse, we expect the people we talk to to speak French themselves.
u11l42.l10: We're also reproached for being stingy, never tipping, and grumbling all the time.
u11l42.l11: But we aren't the biggest grumblers, still.
u11l42.l12: That title goes this year to our British neighbors, who nonetheless rank in the top three best tourists in the world.
u11l42.l13: To close this bouquet of compliments, let's add that we are considered badly-mannered, impolite.
u11l42.l14: There again, we occupy the last rank, just behind the Italians — regularly forgetting the traditional "hello," "thank you," "goodbye."
u11l42.l15: And in this rather dark picture, a small glimmer of hope still: we're in fourth place among the calmest tourists, with the Japanese in first.
u11l42.l16: And we're in third place among the most elegant.

u11l43.l1: The first three people are for the minimum service, the last is against.
u11l43.l2: It's normal that a minimum service be guaranteed, that people can travel and get around at least in emergencies.
u11l43.l3: I think there should... there should be a minimum of trains, not necessarily at peak hours but permanently — a certain number of trains that are guaranteed and allow people to get around in the big cities at least.
u11l43.l4: I'm for the minimum service... uh... quite simply because it penalizes the country when... uh... the SNCF blocks... uh... all the trains on the... uh... French network, so of course I'm for.
u11l43.l5: From the moment there are people willing to guarantee the minimum service, you leave them the choice, and on the other hand you authorize the... the others to go on strike, quite simply — so that there's a... uh... team that is volunteer and... uh... ready to guarantee this minimum service.
u11l43.l7: You have to keep the right to strike so that people can express themselves — that's important; so indeed... uh... the strike must be possible, but you also have to... well, those who aren't striking must have the freedom to be able to work.
u11l43.l8: And so you have to allow that.
u11l43.l9: The minimum service can be a possible alternative.
u11l43.l10: So... uh... things specified in advance, so that people know in advance which trains will be offered, and then, well, in conditions that are obviously... uh... lower frequencies... uh... that kind of thing.
u11l43.l12: There are... there are people who are unhappy, who use the right to strike, which is a... which is a constitutional right, and... uh... calling this constitutional right into question, I think is a dangerous drift — and... uh... the problem is, it's... it's attacking... uh... the consequences without attacking the causes, because people, if they strike, it's not for pleasure... uh... what you need to know is that a day of strike, well, is a day of salary less.
u11l43.l13: And putting in place the minimum service, well... uh...

u4l15.r2: In May 1968, a great protest movement against the established order takes place in France.
u4l15.r3: It begins first in the universities before spreading to the entire country.
u4l15.r4: Violent confrontations with the police take place.
u4l15.r5: Young people protest against authority, want more freedoms in every domain — moral, sexual... — and they make it known through slogans that became famous, like "Under the paving stones, the beach" and "It is forbidden to forbid."
u4l15.r6: May '68 deeply marked French society by freeing its youth from overly strong constraints.
u4l15.r7: Films evoke this period, such as The Dreamers by Italian director Bernardo Bertolucci, released in 2003 with Eva Green, Louis Garrel, Michael Pitt.

u8l30.r1: It's thought that the practice of tipping was born in England.
u8l30.r2: The initial purpose was to encourage the friendliness of waitstaff.
u8l30.r3: This practice arrived later in France.
u8l30.r4: At the very beginning, French customers offered a drink "for drinking," never money.
u8l30.r5: This habit became an obligation, in France, not only for waiters but also for many other professions.
u8l30.r6: "What is always included is the service" — "What one also does if one is pleased with the waiter or waitress is leave a tip." Restaurateurs include the tip in the bill.
u8l30.r9: "Yes, the tip is included by restaurateurs" — this statement is in the passive voice.
u8l30.r10: A brief history of the tip. Widely practiced throughout the world, the tip — or "tip," in English — dates back more than three centuries.
u8l30.r11: The tip finds its origins, apparently, in a restaurant across the Channel whose owner, in the 18th century, set up an original practice.
u8l30.r12: He installed on his counter a jar bearing the inscription "To Insure Promptness."
u8l30.r13: This jar was meant to receive a few coins from customers in a hurry who wished to be served faster.
u8l30.r14: The initials of this expression remained and form the word "tip," now meaning "gratuity" in English.
u8l30.r15: In France, this practice developed later, in the 19th century, to reward café-keepers and restaurateurs for quality service.
u8l30.r16: The word "pourboire" meant, as its name indicates, a drink offered in thanks for a service rendered, or a coin to buy oneself that drink.
u8l30.r17: Over time, this habit diversified and the tip came to be given as a favor granted by a grateful customer to cinema and theater ushers, hairdressers, taxi drivers, hotel staff, and more broadly to many providers of daily or occasional services (home-delivery drivers, repair technicians, tour guides, movers, baggage handlers, couriers, etc.).

u9l35.r16: At 30, Louise Brown, the first "test-tube baby," aspires to... On July 25, 1978, the birth of Louise Brown, the first "test-tube baby," had made headlines across the world.
u9l35.r17: But the Briton, now married and mother of one child, intends to celebrate her 30th birthday discreetly on Friday.
u9l35.r18: While her birth revolutionized the treatment of infertility, allowing millions of couples around the world to conceive through in vitro fertilization (IVF), the young woman is reluctant to celebrate the event in too ostentatious a way.
u9l35.r19: Louise Joy Brown was born by caesarean section on July 25, 1978, at Oldham Hospital, in northwest England.
u9l35.r21: For nine years, her parents Lesley and John had been trying to have a child.
u9l35.r22: But all their attempts had failed until then.
u9l35.r23: They then heard about the work of two Cambridge University doctors, the physiologist Robert Edwards and the gynecologist Patrick Steptoe, who for nearly ten years had been trying to perfect the in vitro fertilization technique.
u9l35.r24: The two researchers combined in a test tube eggs and sperm from Lesley and John Brown, obtaining an embryo that was reimplanted in the mother's uterus, giving birth to the first "test-tube baby."
u9l35.r25: Louise Brown only learned how she had been conceived at age 4, before starting school.
u9l35.r26: "Dad and Mum showed me the video of my birth and tried to explain it to me," she recalls.
u9l35.r27: Her sister Natalie was also born from IVF, four years later.
u9l35.r28: Natalie Brown then became, in 1999, the first "test-tube baby" to give birth — by natural means — to a child.
u9l35.r29: Louise didn't take long to imitate her sister.
u9l35.r30: After marrying Mullinder, a nightclub bouncer, in 2004, she had Cameron in 2006, without any scientific assistance either.
u9l35.r31: Robert Edwards, one of her two scientific "fathers" — Patrick Steptoe died in 1988 — stayed close to the Brown family, even attending Louise's wedding.
u9l35.r32: They met up a few days ago for a ceremony celebrating 30 years of in vitro fertilization at Bourn Hall Clinic, a medical center in eastern England co-founded by Edwards and Steptoe.
u9l35.r33: "Bob (Edwards) is always very busy, but we love seeing him," smiled Louise Brown.
u9l35.r34: "It's good to have such a close relationship.
u9l35.r35: He's like a grandfather to me." LE POINT, Katherine HADDON, 24/07/2008. The harms you do to others won't be long in coming back on you yourself.
